Diy Vs Pro

Is Professional Window Cleaning Worth It? An Honest Assessment for BC Homeowners

Published 2026-05-10 · Wagon Windows

The honest answer is: it depends on your home. For a single-storey rancher with accessible windows and twenty minutes to spare, DIY window cleaning with the right tools is perfectly reasonable. For a two-storey home in Salmon Arm with hard water staining, a ladder-access back deck, and cottonwood pollen covering everything — that's a different calculation entirely.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Get an Instant Estimate (403) 837-5092

The Real Time Cost of DIY Window Cleaning

Most homeowners significantly underestimate how long it takes to clean their windows to a streak-free finish. A medium-sized home with 30 panes inside and out, plus screens, takes an experienced professional roughly 3–4 hours. An inexperienced homeowner working with a bucket, a squeegee they've never used properly, and multiple trips up and down a ladder is looking at 6–8 hours — or more, if they're also dealing with streaks that require a second pass. That time cost is real. At a professional rate of $200–$300 for a full exterior clean on a medium-to-large Salmon Arm home, you're paying roughly $60–75 per hour for someone who brings the right tools, the right technique, and no learning curve. Whether that's worth it is a personal calculation, but most people who try DIY once and then hire a professional don't go back. The technique gap matters more than it sounds. Professional squeegee work leaves glass truly streak-free because the tool is maintained, the solution is correct, and the stroke technique is consistent. Consumer squeegees from hardware stores are usually adequate; the limitation is almost always technique, not equipment. YouTube can teach the basics, but the first few windows will demonstrate the gap between watching and doing.

Ladder Safety: The Argument That Actually Closes the Case

Falls from ladders are the leading cause of serious home injury for adults in Canada. Working on an 8-foot stepladder to reach a standard second-storey window is manageable for most people. Working on a 20-foot extension ladder on uneven ground, while leaning out to reach a window on a hillside lot, while holding a bucket and a squeegee — that's a genuinely dangerous activity for anyone without training and the right equipment. Shuswap homes built on slopes present a particular challenge. What looks like a single-storey home from the street can have a full daylight basement exposed at the back, effectively creating 25+ feet of working height from natural grade. Homes in the Broadview and hillside neighbourhoods of Salmon Arm, and many lake-view properties in Blind Bay and Sorrento, have exactly this configuration. Professional window cleaners work at height routinely and carry the appropriate equipment: commercial extension ladders, standoff brackets for roof overhangs, and in some cases water-fed pole systems that eliminate ladder work on accessible surfaces entirely. They also carry liability insurance that covers them if something does go wrong — a protection you don't have if you're the homeowner on the ladder. This isn't fear-mongering. Experienced homeowners climb ladders safely all the time. But one unlucky step during a window cleaning session creates medical and financial consequences that dwarf the cost of hiring out the job.

  • Falls from ladders are the #1 cause of serious home injury for adults in Canada
  • Second-storey work on hillside lots can reach 20–25 feet
  • Professional cleaners carry insurance; homeowners don't if they're the one who fell

Results: What Pro Cleaning Actually Achieves vs DIY

On a straightforward single-storey window with no mineral buildup, a careful DIYer with the right technique can achieve results comparable to a professional. The gap narrows considerably on accessible, standard windows where time and technique are the only variables. The gap widens dramatically in three situations: hard water staining, screens, and anything requiring ladder access at height. Hard water staining — caused by mineral deposits bonded to the glass surface — cannot be removed by standard cleaning. It requires acid-free treatment products designed to dissolve calcium and silica deposits without damaging the glass. Homeowners who try to scrub mineral deposits off with standard glass cleaner typically either fail to remove them or damage the glass with abrasives. In the Shuswap, lake-adjacent properties and homes on well water with high mineral content are common candidates for this treatment. Screens cleaned by spraying with a garden hose look cleaner but aren't. The fine mesh traps pollen, dust, and organic matter that hosing doesn't dislodge. Professional screen cleaning with a soft-brush wash removes the layer that's actually blocking airflow and contributing to streaky glass. At height, professional results beat DIY almost universally — because the professional can position safely, take their time, and use the right technique. A DIYer on an unstable ladder is focused on not falling, not on squeegee angle.

When DIY Is Actually Fine

Being honest about this matters: there are situations where DIY window cleaning is the sensible choice. If your home is a single-storey rancher with all windows accessible from ground level, you have a good squeegee, the right cleaning solution, and you're comfortable with the technique — there's no strong reason to hire out the work. A standard exterior clean on those terms is a 60–90 minute job. If you clean frequently (every 6–8 weeks during the growing season) and your windows don't have mineral buildup, the maintenance level of dirt is light enough that a quick DIY pass keeps them presentable between professional cleans. Some customers use Wagon Windows for the spring deep clean — which includes screens, tracks, and a thorough first-pass after cottonwood season — and then maintain with DIY touch-ups through summer. What DIY doesn't replace is the periodic professional deep clean that catches buildup before it becomes a damage problem, the safety advantage on anything above ground floor, and the time savings on whole-house cleans that genuinely take most people most of a day.

  • Single-storey, ground-accessible windows: DIY is reasonable
  • Frequent light maintenance between pro cleans: perfectly sensible
  • Multi-storey, hard water, cottonwood buildup: hire a professional

Amortizing the Cost: What Professional Cleaning Costs Per Year

A full-service professional window clean on a medium Salmon Arm home — exterior clean, screens, and tracks — runs roughly $169–$280. If you do this twice a year (spring post-pollen and fall pre-freeze), that's $460–$560 annually. Spread across 12 months, that's roughly $40–$47 per month to have professionally cleaned windows year-round. For most homeowners who think carefully about it, that number is smaller than expected. Consider also what it costs not to clean properly. Mineral deposits that aren't treated eventually etch into the glass permanently. Screens blocked with pollen and organic matter corrode faster. Window frames that aren't wiped down accumulate grime that accelerates paint failure and seal degradation. The cost of regular professional cleaning is, in part, preventive maintenance — cheaper by a wide margin than the glass replacement or repainting it defers.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much time does it realistically take to clean windows yourself on a medium-sized BC home?

Budget a full day for a medium home (30+ panes inside and out, plus screens), including setup, cleaning, and dealing with any streaks on the first pass. If you're inexperienced with a squeegee, add more time. A professional completes the same job in 3–4 hours.

Can I remove hard water stains myself with store-bought products?

Some light mineral haze responds to white vinegar or hardware-store calcium removers. Severe deposits — especially from Shuswap Lake spray or high-mineral well water — typically need professional-grade acid-free treatments applied with specific technique. Abrasive attempts to scrub them off can scratch glass permanently.

Is professional window cleaning safer than DIY on a two-storey home?

Meaningfully so. Professional cleaners have the right ladders, training, and insurance for working at height. Falls from residential ladders cause thousands of serious injuries annually in Canada. For second-storey work, the risk reduction is a genuine argument for hiring out.

How often do I actually need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Most Shuswap homeowners get the best results from three professional cleans: late April/May after cottonwood, mid-summer for dust and pollen, and September before freeze. Lake-adjacent properties may benefit from more frequent service due to mineral spray.

  • hard water stain removal services
